topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of old paper and strong coffee in Dr. Alistair’s office suddenly felt electric, charged with an almost unbearable tension. When he announced his departure, the air in the room seemed to thin, leaving me breathless. I watched the way his fingers traced the rim of his mug, a gesture so casual yet imbued with a profound finality that made my stomach clench. Every word he spoke about moving to a new institution was filtered through a haze of dizzying admiration. I found myself gripping my notebook so tightly my knuckles turned white, desperate to absorb every nuance of his farewell, every lingering glance he sent my way. When he finally stood to leave, the space he vacated felt vast and suddenly cold. I wanted to speak, to ask him what this meant for our work, but only a dry, small sound escaped my throat. I just stood there, my gaze fixed on the door, feeling a strange, hollow ache bloom beneath my ribs.

[story 2]

I kept catching myself staring at the worn leather of his desk chair, tracing the lines with my eyes as he packed his things. The news—that Dr. Hemlock was leaving—hit me like a physical blow, stealing the oxygen from the room. My hands began to tremble slightly as I tried to gather my scattered notes, the movement clumsy and jerky. When he offered a quick, warm smile, a genuine expression that softened the sharp edges of his usual academic demeanor, I felt a sudden, overwhelming urge to close the distance between us. I leaned forward, ostensibly to hand him a stack of printed materials, but the proximity made my breath catch. His eyes, when they met mine, held a depth I hadn't noticed before, a startling intensity that made my cheeks flush a deep crimson. "Thank you, Dr. Hemlock," I managed, my voice sounding unexpectedly shaky, a little too loud in the quiet office.

[story 3]

The announcement echoed in the hallway, a dull thud against the polished linoleum floor. For a long moment, I simply stood, rooted to the spot, watching the figure of my advisor gather his belongings. A strange warmth spread through my chest, a fluttering sensation that made my focus entirely dissipate from the impending academic fallout. I watched the way he adjusted his tie, a small, unconscious movement that seemed incredibly significant. I wanted to say something intelligent, something profound about the trajectory of my research, but my mind was a blank slate, filled only with the image of him—the way the light caught the silver in his hair, the subtle curve of his jaw. When he finally turned toward the door, pausing just before exiting, I felt an undeniable pull, a desperate need to be near that moment, to somehow anchor myself to his presence before he vanished.
